narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 58.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, well below the typical student's 6th decile, a Year 3 decile of 1, even lower than the average 6th decile, an undergraduate GPA of 2.90 compared to the usual 3.21, and a solid LSAT score of 38 that beats the dataset average of 37. You're from an upper-middle income family, identify as male—which is common among applicants—and White, the most frequent racial background in our data at over 80%, and you plan to attend full-time like nearly all students. The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your Year 3 decile, which had the biggest influence and pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure rather than success. Right behind it in impact was your Year 1 decile, also steering the model toward a failure outlook. Your LSAT score came next in influence, contributing in the direction of failure for your specific case despite being above average. Family income followed closely, with its upper-middle level nudging the prediction similarly toward risk. Your undergraduate GPA played a notable role too, reinforcing the failure signal given its below-average mark. Less influential but still pushing that way were your racial background, gender, and full-time status, which align with typical applicants but didn't counterbalance the stronger factors. Overall, your lower academic deciles in school years stood out as the top drivers compared to peers who generally rank higher. To boost your future chances, focus on reversing those low deciles by excelling in law school coursework through better study strategies or tutoring, as stronger grades are realistic to improve and directly signal bar readiness. Building on your good LSAT, targeted bar prep courses could also help demonstrate reliability where your profile shows vulnerability. We encourage reapplying after addressing these areas—you have strengths to build on.
